content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 8 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_008_page_001.jpg

### Food Offered to Idols

1. Now regarding your question about food that has been offered to idols. Yes, we know that "we all have knowledge" about this issue. But while knowledge makes us feel important, it is love that strengthens the church.

2. Anyone who claims to know all the answers doesn't really know very much.

3. But the person who loves God is the one whom God recognizes.

4. So, what about eating meat that has been offered to idols? Well, we all know that an idol is not really a god and that there is only one God.

5. There may be so-called gods both in heaven and on earth, and some people actually worship many gods and many lords.

6. But for us, there is one God, the Father, who created everything, and we live for him. And there is one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom God made everything and through whom we have been given life.

7. However, not all believers know this. Some are accustomed to thinking of idols as being real, so when they eat food that has been offered to idols, they think of it as the worship of real gods, and their weak consciences are violated.

8. It's true that we can't win God's approval by what we eat. We don't lose anything if we don't eat it, and we don't gain anything if we do.

9. But you must be careful so that your freedom does not cause others with a weaker conscience to stumble.

10. For if others see you — with your "superior knowledge" — eating in the temple of an idol, won't they be encouraged to violate their conscience by eating food that has been offered to an idol?

11. So because of your superior knowledge, a weak believer is destroyed — someone for whom Christ died.

12. And when you sin against other believers by encouraging them to do something they believe is wrong, you are sinning against Christ.

13. So if what I eat causes another believer to sin, I will never eat meat again as long as I live — for I don't want to cause another believer to stumble.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The situation:** Corinth was a city saturated with pagan temples. Almost every social event took place at a temple — business dinners, family celebrations, guild meetings. The food served had been ritually offered to a god. New Christians faced a daily dilemma: can I eat this food? Some "strong" believers said yes — idols aren't real, so the food is just food. Some "weak" believers said no — eating it feels like worshiping the old gods. Paul sides with the strong in theory and the weak in practice.

**Verse 1 — "Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up":** This is the thesis of the entire section (chapters 8-10). The Corinthians loved knowledge. They prided themselves on theological sophistication. Paul agrees they're right — idols are nothing. But being right isn't enough. If your correct theology leads you to destroy a fellow believer, your theology has failed. Love trumps knowledge. Always.

**Verse 6 — The Christian creed:** Tucked into a discussion about meat is one of the most important theological statements in the New Testament. Paul takes the Jewish Shema — "Hear, O Israel, the Lord our God, the Lord is one" — and expands it to include Jesus. One God, the Father. One Lord, Jesus Christ. And Christ is the one "through whom" all things exist. In a single verse, Paul places Jesus within the identity of Israel's God. This is not a deviation from monotheism — it is monotheism redefined.

**Verse 11 — "Someone for whom Christ died":** This is the knockout punch. You want to exercise your freedom to eat temple food? Fine. But look at the person next to you. That person with the weak conscience — Christ died for them. Your theological correctness is not worth more than their faith. If your freedom destroys someone Christ died for, you are working against the cross.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
